attributes.stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a one-month history of double vision and a drooping right eyelid. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His right eyelid is partially drooped. When looking at a single image, he reports that the horizontal images separate further when looking to the left. The left-most (nasal field of vision) image disappears when the right eye is covered when looking at this single image.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following nerves is most likely to be impaired in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a one-month history of binocular diplopia. The presence of binocular diplopia, paired with the presence of right sided ptosis suggests right oculomotor nerve dysfunction; in this case an incomplete right oculomotor nerve palsy. The oculomotor nerve has three main motor functions:

  • Upper eyelid (somatic innervation)

  • Eye muscles (visual tracking and gaze fixation) (somatic innervation)

  • Pupil and lens (autonomic, parasympathetic innervation)

A useful initial step in the workup of diplopia is to establish whether the diplopia is monocular (e.g., due to refractive issue) or binocular (e.g., due to visual misalignment). This is established by covering one eye; binocular diplopia is caused by misalignment of the visual axes and consequently resolves when one is covered whereas, monocular diplopia does not typically resolve under these circumstances.

The next step is to ascertain whether the diplopia is separated in the horizontal or vertical plane. Purely horizontal diplopia is most likely due to impaired function or control of the medial and/or lateral rectus muscles (either singular or together). Oblique separation with tilting of an image is indicative of the impaired function of the inferior or superior oblique muscles. The direction of gaze which worsens the separation is also helpful; typically, images are maximally separated when gazing into the functional area of the paretic muscle.

An oculomotor palsy can result from a lesion at any place between the oculomotor nucleus (within the midbrain) and the extraocular muscles. A complete oculomotor palsy has a classical “down and out” appearance due to unopposed function of the lateral rectus (CN VI) and superior oblique (CN IV) and is typically accompanied by a “blown (large) pupil” with full ptosis due to interrupted innervation of the sphincter pupillae and levator palpebrae superioris. Interestingly, the oculomotor nerve divides into two subdivisions within the superior orbital fissure:

  • The superior branch innervates to the superior rectus and levator palpebrae superioris.

  • The inferior branch innervates the inferior rectus, medial rectus, and inferior oblique.

As such, there may be complete or incomplete involvement of the extraocular muscles innervated by the oculomotor nerve which results in a variable degree of ptosis as well as weakness of ipsilateral the inferior and medial rectus as well as the superior rectus and inferior obliques.

The patient above reports worsening of his diplopia on left lateral gaze which involves the use of lateral rectus controlled by CN VI (left eye) and medial rectus controlled by CN III (right eye) which when working results in bi-foveal fixation of an object. These movements are known as vergence eye movements and act to ensure that that the image of the object is projected onto both foveae, irrespective of distance. In the event of a paretic right oculomotor nerve (or inferior division) while in left gaze, the medial rectus cannot adduct the right eye resulting in the image striking the retina lateral to the fovea. The diagram shows a right medical rectus palsy, leading to misalignment of the visual axes (Image). As bi-foveal fixation is impaired, the diplopia that develops results in the false image perceived as “lateral” to the real object (as per the figure). This explains why the above patient's “leftmost image” disappears when the right eye is covered.

It's important to note that oculomotor nerve palsies can have various causes, including microvascular ischemia (especially in patients with diabetes or hypertension), compression (e.g., from aneurysms or tumors), or inflammatory conditions. Further investigation, including neuroimaging, may be warranted to determine the underlying cause of this patient's oculomotor nerve palsy.

The left abducens nerve (cranial nerve VI) innervates the lateral rectus muscle and its palsy would cause inward deviation of the affected eye with diplopia maximal on ipsilateral gaze. This doesn't fit with the described symptoms.

The right abducens nerve palsy would cause diplopia maximal on right gaze, which is not consistent with this patient's presentation.

A left oculomotor nerve palsy would cause left-sided ptosis and diplopia maximal on right gaze, which doesn't match this patient's symptoms.

The right facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) is responsible for facial muscle movement, including closure of the eyelid, but not for lifting it. A facial nerve palsy would not explain the diplopia or the ptosis seen in this patient.
learningPoints: Subjective diplopia should be tested by sequentially occluding each eye; the general rule is that loss of the outermost image comes from covering the affected eye.
Monocular diplopia suggests a primary ocular cause. Binocular diplopia suggests a neurological cause.
Right oculomotor nerve palsy typically presents with ptosis, diplopia worse on left gaze, and possible pupillary involvement.
references: Danchaivijitr C, Kennard C. Diplopia and eye movement disorders. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 2004 Dec;75 Suppl 4(Suppl 4):iv24-31. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1765677/
Lee AG. Third cranial nerve (oculomotor nerve) palsy in adults. In: Uptodate [Online]. 2021. Available at: https://sso.uptodate.com/contents/third-cranial-nerve-oculomotor-nerve-palsy-in-adults

Lavin P. Neuro-ophthalmology: Ocular Motor System. In: Neupsy Key [online]. 2021. Available at: https://neupsykey.com/neuro-ophthalmology-ocular-motor-system/#:~:text=The%20ocular%20motor%20system%20places,of%20the%20visual%20axes%20(Fig.

attributes.instructions: Hi Dhiren, hope you're well! this is an item you previously reviewed for us (thank you). A user has asked for more description of the image included. Noting this is a difficult question for students, I was wondering if you could suggest a few lines to help with understanding the image? Thank you!
